name: Residential Purchase Agreement Summary for Title & Escrow
summary: This skill instructs an LLM to analyze a fully executed residential purchase agreement package—including all addenda, amendments, and counteroffers—and produce a structured, scannable operational summary for title company closers and processors. It extracts all deal-critical terms including parties, property identifiers, price and earnest money, financing, contingencies, title and survey provisions, HOA and assessment details, closing cost allocations, prorations, and possession terms, presenting them in organized tables with source pointers. The skill flags missing information, document conflicts, unusual clauses, and tight or passed deadlines without offering legal advice, and requires human review before reliance.
content:
# Residential Purchase Agreement Summary for Title & Escrow (US)

## 1. Opening Context

In the lifecycle of a residential real estate transaction, the title company acts as the neutral clearinghouse and fiduciary for all parties. The Residential Purchase Agreement (RPA) is not merely a contract between a buyer and seller; it is the primary source of instruction for the title company's entire closing workflow. This skill transforms a dense, often messy legal contract into a surgical, scannable roadmap that a title closer or processor can use to open a file, order title, clear requirements, coordinate payoffs, schedule closing, and prepare the settlement statement.

The output is not a legal memo and not a contract interpretation. It is a high-reliability "term sheet" derived from the signed residential purchase agreement and its addenda, with explicit flags for missing information, unusual or non-standard provisions, and deadlines that are tight or already passed. Execution requires a high degree of precision because even minor oversights—failing to note a seller credit, missing a tight earnest money deadline, overlooking a controlling amendment that changed the purchase price—can result in delayed closings, financial liability for the title company, or a breach of escrow instructions. A superior summary does not just repeat the contract's words; it organizes the contract's mechanical requirements so that a processor can identify every required action item at a glance, from ordering a payoff to verifying HOA status, without reading the entire agreement.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

The title company serves as the bridge between the contract's intent and the actual transfer of property. The purpose of this summary is to decode the Purchase Agreement into actionable data points aligned with the title agency's workflow. For a title closer, the contract is a list of conditions precedent to closing. This document functions as the operational equivalent of escrow instructions, dictating how funds are to be held, how costs are to be split, and when the property may legally change hands.

The summary serves three primary internal stakeholders. The Closer and Processor use it to prepare the settlement statement (ALTA or Closing Disclosure), verify that all conditions are met, and identify what must be ordered or cleared next. The Lender relies on the title company to ensure the contract terms match the loan underwrite, particularly regarding seller credits and financing contingencies. The Real Estate Agents depend on the title company to track critical dates and coordinate scheduling. By flagging unusual clauses—such as post-closing occupancy agreements, non-standard credit structures, or trust sellers requiring additional documentation—the title company protects itself from being caught in a dispute between buyer and seller at the closing table.

This summary also plays an important coordination role even in a neutral posture. Buyer and seller (and their agents) may disagree about what the contract requires. The lender may impose requirements not reflected in the agreement. Addenda may alter deadlines or costs in ways that contradict earlier documents. The summary must therefore be rigorously source-grounded: every extracted term should include a pointer to where it appears (page, section, or document label), and paraphrases must not change meaning. When the agreement is ambiguous or internally inconsistent—for example, two different closing dates appearing in different addenda—the skill must not resolve the conflict. It must flag it prominently and require human review.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before generating the summary, the executing LLM must obtain the complete agreement package and enough context to compute and flag deadlines. A title file cannot be accurately summarized based on an incomplete or draft agreement. At minimum, the LLM must have the fully executed residential purchase agreement, including all pages, exhibits, riders, and signature blocks with signatures from all buyers and all sellers. It must also have all addenda and amendments referenced in the agreement, including but not limited to financing addenda, inspection addenda, appraisal addenda, well and septic addenda, lead-based paint disclosures for pre-1978 housing, FHA or VA addenda if applicable, seller concession addenda, escalation addenda, and any attorney riders. Any counteroffers incorporated by reference must be included. The LLM also needs the effective date (often defined as the date of last signature or acceptance) and any defined "business day" convention in the contract. Where available, earnest money receipts, MLS sheets, or closing instructions may be provided for cross-checking addresses and names.

If any of these documents are missing—particularly the legal description, signed amendments referenced in the main body, or execution signatures—the LLM must explicitly flag this for the user immediately rather than proceeding with incomplete data. If the effective date is not explicit, the LLM must ask: "What is the contract effective date (acceptance date or last signature date)?" If the user cannot provide it, the summary should still extract all stated dates, but deadline calculations must be flagged as "cannot compute—effective date not confirmed." The LLM should also ask what state the property is in and whether the title team wants state-specific headings while still producing a jurisdiction-agnostic baseline summary.

## 4. Step-by-Step Process

### Step 1: Intake, Document Normalization, and Scope Control

The skill begins by confirming its scope: the output is an operational summary for title and escrow processing. It does not opine on enforceability, interpret ambiguous legal language, or advise whether a party is in default. This boundary must be stated at the top of every output.

Next, normalize the document set. Residential transactions often include multiple PDFs, scanned pages, and addenda with overlapping fields. The LLM should identify every distinct instrument in the package—for example, "Purchase Agreement," "Financing Addendum," "Inspection Addendum," "Counteroffer #1," "Amendment #2"—and assign each a short label used later for source references. If the PDF includes page numbers, preserve them; if not, use a stable internal page index (such as "PDF page 7 of 23") and note that numbering is based on the provided file.

Quality execution here means resisting the common mistake of extracting only from the first "purchase agreement" pages while ignoring addenda that supersede terms. Many markets routinely change price, closing date, possession, or seller contributions in later amendments. The LLM must treat amendments as potentially controlling and must flag when a later document modifies a key term previously extracted.

The LLM must also verify the presence of signatures and initials where the form expects them and flag "execution incomplete" when signature blocks are blank, when an addendum appears unsigned, or when initials are missing on a critical page such as a price-change amendment. An unsigned contract is not a valid instruction for the title company.

### Step 2: Extract Parties, Vesting-Relevant Details, and Contacts

The LLM must look beyond the names on the first page. It must extract the buyer(s) and seller(s) exactly as written, including middle initials, suffixes, entity designations (LLC, Inc., trust), and any "and/or assigns" language. For title work, the exact spelling and entity type is operationally critical because it affects vesting, deed preparation, and underwriting requirements. If the buyer or seller is a trust, the LLM must extract the full trust name and any trustee names shown, because the title processor will need to request trust documents for the title commitment. If the agreement provides mailing addresses, emails, or phone numbers, extract them into a contacts section, but do not invent missing data.

The LLM must also check the signature blocks to confirm consistency. If a spouse is missing from a signature block in what may be a community property state, or if a corporate entity is signing without an officer title (such as "Manager"), these are potential title hurdles that must be flagged. The processor must also verify that the seller named in the contract matches the owner of record; if there is any reason to suspect a mismatch, flag it as a potential chain-of-title issue.

Broker and agent information should be extracted separately if present, because title processors often coordinate scheduling and document requests through agents. However, the summary must clearly distinguish "party" from "agent" to avoid confusion. Common mistakes include silently truncating long entity names, dropping "as trustee," treating an LLC buyer like an individual, or misspelling names, which affects lien searches. When names differ between documents (such as the purchase agreement and a financing addendum), flag the discrepancy as critical.

Community property or marital status descriptors that appear in some state forms should be captured as written without translating them into legal conclusions. The flag should read: "Vesting/marital status language present—confirm deed vesting instructions."

### Step 3: Extract Property Identifiers and Flag Title-Search Blockers

For title work, the most important extraction is property identity. The LLM must extract the street address (including unit number), city, state, and zip code, the county (if stated), the parcel ID, APN, or PIN, and any legal description text included or incorporated by reference (such as "Lot 3, Block 2, Sunny Acres…"). If the agreement says "legal to govern" or attaches an exhibit for the legal description, record that reference and flag if the exhibit is missing from the provided documents.

If the agreement includes only an address and omits both parcel ID and legal description, flag this as a title-search risk. Address-only identification is often insufficient for reliable search, especially for rural properties, condominiums with limited common elements, or multi-parcel transactions. A property described only as "my house on Main St" without a legal description may not satisfy the Statute of Frauds, and title may not be insurable on that basis alone.

If the agreement references additional parcels, outlots, common elements, storage units, dock rights, or parking stalls, flag each for title follow-up and note that ordering of condominium documents or plat review may be required. If the agreement mentions included personal property or fixtures (appliances, window treatments, a tractor), capture the inclusion and exclusion list because it can affect lender expectations, settlement statement entries, and whether a Bill of Sale is required, but keep it secondary to title identifiers.

### Step 4: Extract the Financial Architecture

The LLM must break down the flow of funds with precision. Extract the purchase price and break out all monetary components that affect escrow receipts and settlement statement preparation. For earnest money deposits (EMD), identify three things for each deposit: the amount, who is holding it (broker, escrow company, or title company), and the deadline for deposit along with the method (check or wire). If the title company holds the EMD, the closer must track receipt immediately. If there are multiple deposits (initial EMD plus additional EMD), extract each separately with its trigger and deadline.

Analyze the financing structure. Is the transaction cash, conventional, FHA, or VA? This dictates the character of the closing. FHA and VA loans often have mandatory seller-paid costs that cannot be negotiated away, and the title company must account for these on the Closing Disclosure. Extract the loan amount or maximum loan-to-value ratio if provided, the down payment, whether the buyer must apply by a certain date, and the deadline for obtaining commitment or approval.

Extract seller concessions, closing cost credits, repair credits, rate buy-down credits, and any allowances. These often appear in addenda or counteroffers and may conflict with lender caps depending on loan type. The skill must not advise on compliance; it should flag "seller credit present—confirm lender acceptability and disclosure requirements" without stating what the law requires. The LLM must distinguish between credits for repairs and general closing cost credits because lenders often treat them differently.

In states like Texas, the "Option Fee" is a distinct payment made directly to the seller (not to escrow) that gives the buyer an unrestricted right to terminate during the option period. The LLM must not conflate option fees with earnest money. When the contract uses escalation clauses, extract the escalation terms and note whether the price has been adjusted. When multiple documents state different prices, present a "Price History / Conflicts" notation showing each stated price with its source and flag it as requiring human reconciliation.

### Step 5: Map the Critical Timeline

Real estate transactions live and die by the calendar. The LLM must construct a chronological timeline of every deadline extracted from the agreement and all addenda. This includes the effective or acceptance date, EMD deposit deadline(s), inspection period start and end, repair request and resolution deadlines, appraisal contingency date, financing application deadline, financing commitment or approval deadline, title commitment delivery deadline, buyer's title objection deadline, seller's cure period, survey deadline, HOA document delivery and review periods, closing date, and possession date and time.

When calculating dates, the LLM must apply the logic found in the contract's definitions section. Does the contract count calendar days or business days? Does the clock start on the effective date or the day after? If the contract references "business days" without defining them, flag the deadline computation as uncertain. If today's date is available in the system context, the LLM should flag deadlines as Passed, Due within 48 hours, Due within 7 days, or Future, and should include the computation basis (such as "Effective Date January 10, 2026 plus 10 calendar days"). If today's date is not available, omit status logic and instead note "Cannot assess tightness—current date not provided."

The LLM must flag any closing date that falls on a Sunday or federal holiday as an immediate scheduling conflict. It must note "Time is of the Essence" language if present, as this makes deadlines strictly enforceable rather than aspirational. Common errors include ignoring that amendments reset deadlines, failing to capture time-of-day possession terms, computing deadlines when the effective date is ambiguous, and collapsing multiple inspection-related deadlines (inspection, notice to cure, re-inspection) into one when they should be tracked separately.

### Step 6: Extract Title, Survey, HOA, and Assessment Provisions

This step focuses on the specific requirements for the title commitment and related items that are core to the title company's operations. Extract who selects the title company or escrow holder (if stated), whether an owner's title insurance policy is required and who pays, any requirement for an ALTA survey or existing survey (noting who pays and the deadline to order it, since surveys are often the longest-lead item), and any specific title standards referenced (such as "marketable title," "insurable title," or "ALTA owner's policy"). If the agreement references particular permitted exceptions (easements, covenants, mineral rights), capture them.

Capture the title commitment timeline: when the seller must provide evidence of title or commitment, the buyer's period to object to title defects, and the seller's cure period. If there is a right to terminate based on title objections, extract the notice mechanics and deadlines.

For HOA and condominium properties, extract whether the property is subject to an HOA or condo association, whether resale disclosures, bylaws, budgets, or CIC documents must be provided, who pays HOA transfer fees, and any buyer review or termination period. Title companies are responsible for obtaining HOA estoppels or clearance letters, and if the buyer has a review period after receiving HOA documents, the title company must know exactly when those documents were delivered to start the clock. Flag "HOA indicated but documents or fee allocation unclear" when the agreement suggests an association but does not specify details.

For special assessments, extract any clause allocating responsibility for pending or existing special assessments, municipal improvements, or "levied but not yet due" assessments. If the contract is silent, flag "special assessments allocation not stated—confirm local custom and closing instructions." If a municipality has a pending assessment for infrastructure and the contract does not address it, the title company may face a surprise post-closing dispute. Do not fill gaps with assumptions.

If a home warranty provision appears, note the cost and provider; this is a line item that must appear on the final settlement statement. Neglecting to list a home warranty is a frequent last-minute correction that delays the signing of the closing package.

### Step 7: Extract Closing Cost Allocations, Prorations, Possession, and Special Provisions

For closing costs, extract who pays for the owner's policy, lender's policy, escrow or closing fee, recording fees, transfer taxes or documentary stamps, survey, pest inspection, home warranty, and any required repairs or certifications. These allocations vary significantly by jurisdiction. If the contract uses a general clause (such as "split equally") plus specific exceptions, capture both layers. Settlement statement accuracy depends on capturing these allocations precisely.

For prorations, extract whether taxes, rents, utilities, HOA dues, and assessments are prorated, and the proration basis (calendar year versus fiscal year, current year taxes versus last available). If the contract contains a tax proration method clause, extract it verbatim because it directly affects credits and escrows on the settlement statement.

For possession, extract the possession date and time, occupancy or rent-back terms, security deposit amounts, daily rent figures, holdback provisions, and utility transfer responsibilities if stated. Flag "post-closing possession" as a heightened operational risk requiring additional documentation (a post-closing occupancy agreement and potentially an escrow holdback).

For special provisions and unusual clauses, extract all handwritten terms, "Additional Terms" or "Special Stipulations" sections, builder addenda clauses that shift risk, and any non-standard clauses about arbitration, mediation, attorney fees, limitation of remedies, or specific performance. Look for anything that deviates from a standard residential transaction: a tenant in the property, a solar panel lease that needs to be assumed, personal property requiring a Bill of Sale, or a sale contingent on the buyer selling their current home. The output must not interpret enforceability; it should flag "non-standard clause—attorney or underwriter review recommended" for provisions that deviate from typical form language.

Finally, produce a "Title & Escrow Follow-Up Items" section that translates all flags into actionable requests organized by priority. Examples include: "Need executed amendment referenced in contract but not provided," "Need legal description exhibit," "Need HOA contact and transfer fee amounts," "Confirm EMD holder and receipt date," "Confirm lender name and loan type," and "Post-closing occupancy agreement required." This is operational triage, not legal instruction.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

This skill is designed for non-attorney title operations, but it must respect legal ethics and risk controls. The output must be framed as document extraction and risk flagging only, not legal advice.

The most important compliance boundary is the unauthorized practice of law (UPL). Title processors are not attorneys, and this summary must remain a factual extraction that does not offer legal opinions on the enforceability of clauses or the rights of parties. Under ABA Model Rule 5.5, non-lawyers may not engage in the practice of law. Any complex legal conflict identified in the contract should be flagged with the note: "Refer to underwriter or legal counsel for interpretation." The LLM must avoid statements like "buyer may terminate" unless directly quoting the contract, and even then should frame it as "Contract states: [quoted language]" rather than as advice.

The summary should align with American Land Title Association (ALTA) best practices, particularly regarding the accuracy of settlement statements and the handling of escrow funds. ALTA's best practices framework provides guidance on title and settlement procedures that title companies follow across the country.

For financed transactions, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau's TILA-RESPA Integrated Disclosure (TRID) rule mandates strict timing for the Closing Disclosure. The title company must have the final contract terms well in advance of closing to meet the three-business-day delivery requirement to the consumer before consummation. Any summary produced within five days of a closing date should be flagged as "High Priority—TRID timing risk." The skill must not generate TRID disclosures or give compliance conclusions; it should flag "lender-required credit present—confirm disclosure requirements" and leave compliance determinations to the appropriate professionals.

The title company has a fiduciary duty to follow the written instructions of both parties. If the summary misses an instruction—such as a specific credit or a required holdback—the title company may be liable for the resulting loss. This reinforces the need for completeness and traceability in every extraction.

Confidentiality and privacy are paramount. Purchase agreements include personal data including names, addresses, and sometimes contact information and financial terms. The LLM must treat all inputs and outputs as confidential. If sensitive data such as Social Security numbers or account numbers appears in the documents, the LLM must mask them and flag "sensitive data present—do not distribute."

The executing LLM must never fabricate statutes, regulations, case citations, or legal authorities. If the user asks for legal authority or jurisdiction-specific requirements, the LLM must either perform a web search and provide verifiable URLs or explicitly state that attorney verification is required. No legal citation should be presented without verification.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

Quality review begins with traceability. Every extracted term must have a source pointer identifying the document label and section or page. If a term is not explicitly present, the correct output is "Not stated" or "Not found," never a guess. The summary must be internally consistent: the purchase price should match the sum of price components where applicable; the closing date should match across the document set or be flagged as a conflict; and the EMD due date should not post-date closing without a flag.

Apply a direct citation check for every dollar amount. If the original contract states $500,000 but Amendment 2 states $480,000, the summary must reflect $480,000 and note the change. Recalculate any date that is stated as a day-count formula (such as "45 days after effective date") into the actual calendar date when the effective date is known, and verify it does not fall on a weekend or holiday. Confirm that all parties who are supposed to sign have actually signed and initialed every page where required; missing initials on a price-change page can invalidate the term.

Validate completeness against title operations needs. The summary must contain at minimum: parties' full legal names and entity types, property address and at least one additional identifier (parcel ID or legal description reference), purchase price and EMD logistics, financing type and key deadlines (if financed), closing and possession dates, title objection and commitment timeline, HOA and assessment handling, and closing cost allocations. If any of these is missing, the summary must elevate the missing item into the follow-up queue.

Apply the "Closing Day" test: if the closer were sitting at the table today, would anything in this file surprise them? If there is a mention of a tenant in the property, a solar panel lease, or an unresolved repair escrow that is not in the summary, the summary is incomplete.

The output must include the following disclaimer, used verbatim: "Operational summary for title and escrow processing only. Not legal advice. Verify all terms against the fully executed purchase agreement, counteroffers, amendments, and addenda. Flagged items require human review before reliance."

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

This skill is intentionally US-wide and form-agnostic, but it should support customization along the axis of state form families and customary allocations. The main structural schema—Parties, Property, Price and EMD, Financing, Dates, Title and Survey, HOA and Assessments, Costs and Prorations, Possession, Addenda and Amendments, Special Provisions, and Flags and Follow-Up—works across all jurisdictions.

When a state is identified, the LLM may add a brief state-specific notes subsection that addresses only what the contract itself references. The following illustrative examples demonstrate the kinds of variations the LLM should watch for. In California (C.A.R. forms), attention should be paid to liquidated damages and arbitration initials, because if the buyer does not initial these provisions, the escrow instructions for EMD disputes change. The Natural Hazard Disclosure requirement should also be tracked. In Texas (TREC forms), the Option Fee in Paragraph 5 is unique; it is paid directly to the seller rather than to escrow and grants the buyer an unrestricted right to terminate. The title company must confirm receipt of the earnest money but typically not the option fee. In Florida (FAR/BAR forms), the distinction between the "As-Is" contract and the standard contract matters because the "As-Is" version has a specific inspection-and-terminate clause that determines when the EMD becomes non-refundable. In New York and other northeastern states, an attorney review period often means the contract is not binding until attorneys approve it, and the title file should not be fully opened until that period has cleared. In Minnesota, statutory cancellation rights and specific requirements for well and septic disclosures may trigger buyer rescission rights.

In all cases, the LLM should identify the specific state form being used (such as "TREC 20-17" or "C.A.R. RPA-CA") as this helps the processor know which boilerplate provisions apply. If the user requests state-law explanations beyond what the contract itself states, the LLM must either perform web research with verifiable URLs or mark the content as requiring attorney verification. The LLM must not supply customary rules from memory without verification.

---

## Output Specification

The LLM's final output must be a structured, quick-scanning summary using headings, tables, and concise entries. The tone must be operational and neutral, written for title closers and processors.

The output must include the following sections, in order:

**Header.** Include File or Order Name (if provided), Property Address, State (if known), list of documents received, Effective Date (as stated), Summary Date, and the required disclaimer.

**Deal Snapshot.** A two-column summary table with: Buyer(s), Seller(s), Property, Purchase Price, Total EMD, Closing Date, Possession, Loan Type and Amount (or Cash), Title Company or Escrow Holder (if stated), HOA (Yes, No, or Unclear), Special Assessments (Noted or Not stated), and count of Key Flags.

**Parties and Contacts.** Separate tables for parties and for agents, brokers, and other contacts. Include name as written, entity type, signing status, and source reference.

**Property Details.** Address, unit, city/state/zip, county, parcel or APN, legal description (verbatim or "See Exhibit __"), additional parcels or outlots, personal property included or excluded, and source. Flag prominently if legal description or parcel ID is missing.

**Price, Earnest Money, and Credits.** Purchase price, escalation or price adjustment terms, each EMD deposit with amount, due date, holder, method, and application to price, seller concessions and credits, repair credits and allowances, option fee if present, and source.

**Financing and Appraisal.** Financing type, loan amount, down payment, lender name, application deadline, commitment deadline, financing contingency date, appraisal contingency terms, appraisal objection deadline, and source.

**Contingencies.** Inspection period dates, notice deadlines, repair request and resolution deadlines, home sale contingency details, and any other contingencies (well/septic, radon, lead-based paint, insurance) with deadlines and source.

**Title, Survey, and Closing Documentation.** Title commitment delivery deadline, buyer objection period, seller cure period, permitted exceptions, survey requirement and deadline, who pays for owner's and lender's policy, escrow or closing location selection, and source.

**HOA and Condominium.** Whether HOA or condo association applies, association name and contact, document delivery requirements, buyer review period, transfer fee responsibility, dues proration, and source.

**Closing Date, Possession, and Occupancy.** Closing date, scheduled time, possession date and time, rent-back or leaseback terms, per diem rent, holdback or security deposit, and source.

**Closing Costs, Taxes, and Prorations.** Allocation of escrow fee, recording fees, transfer taxes, survey, inspections, home warranty, title premiums, tax proration method, HOA dues proration, special assessments allocation, and source.

**Addenda, Amendments, and Disclosures Inventory.** Each document listed with name, date, signing status, what it modifies, and source page range.

**Critical Dates and Deadlines.** A table with columns for Item, Trigger, Deadline as written, Computed date (if calculable), Status (Passed, Due within 48 hours, Due within 7 days, Future, or Unknown), Notes, and Source. Compute only when the effective date and day-count convention are clear; otherwise leave computed date blank and mark status Unknown.

**Flags and Follow-Up Items.** Organized by severity: Critical (blocking file opening or closing), High (likely to delay closing), Medium (needs clarification), and Low (informational). Each item must state what is missing or unusual, why it matters operationally, and where to look in the documents.

**Source Map.** A table mapping each major category to the primary document label and page or section.

## Execution Guardrails

The LLM must never fabricate missing values. If a field is not present, use "Not stated" and add a follow-up flag. The LLM must not resolve conflicts between documents; it must display conflicts side-by-side and flag them for human resolution. The LLM must avoid legal conclusions. Use "Contract states…" or "Agreement provides…" and keep quoted passages short. If the user asks for legal requirements or enforceability analysis, the LLM must respond that attorney review is required and offer to perform web research with verifiable URLs if the user wants jurisdiction-specific information. The LLM must protect sensitive data by including only what is needed for processing and masking any Social Security numbers or account numbers if detected.

skill_references.name: Title Commitment Analysis & Objection Drafter
skill_references.summary: Takes the purchase agreement summary and compares it against the title commitment to identify necessary curative actions such as liens, easements, and encumbrances.

skill_references.name: CD/ALTA Settlement Statement Auditor
skill_references.summary: Verifies the final settlement numbers against the purchase agreement summary to ensure all credits, prorations, and cost allocations are accurately reflected.

skill_references.name: Escrow Instructions Drafting
skill_references.summary: Converts purchase agreement terms into formal escrow instructions for the title company's internal use.

skill_references.name: Real Estate Amendment & Addendum Impact Analyzer
skill_references.summary: Compares amendments and addenda against the base purchase agreement summary to highlight changes in price, dates, credits, and other key terms.

skill_references.name: HOA Disclosure and Estoppel Review
skill_references.summary: Reviews HOA documents and estoppel letters to identify hidden fees, assessment obligations, and requirements that affect the closing.

skill_references.name: Neutral Professional Tone
skill_references.summary: Guidance on maintaining objective, factual tone appropriate for neutral-party title and escrow operations.
